claim: Are vaccinated and boosted people more susceptible to infection or disease with the omicron variant than unvaccinated people?

result.claim: Are vaccinated and boosted people more susceptible to infection or disease with the omicron variant than unvaccinated people?
result.reasoning: The initial search for propositions and chunks related to the claim yielded one potentially relevant paper (Paper ID 51). However, the abstract of this paper indicates it investigates the effectiveness of booster doses in *reducing* SARS-CoV-2 infections, not an increased susceptibility in vaccinated/boosted individuals. A targeted search within this paper for terms related to the claim also returned no results. Other search results were not relevant to the claim. Therefore, there is currently insufficient evidence in the knowledge base to either support or refute the claim that vaccinated and boosted people are more susceptible to infection or disease with the omicron variant than unvaccinated people.

evidence.text: Exposure to FLX, SER and BUP showed increased uptake in brain tissues.
evidence.source: Selective uptake and biological consequences of environmentally relevant antidepressant pharmaceutical exposures on male fathead minnows

evidence.text: The study assessed the safety of the BNT162b2 mRNA vaccination.
evidence.source: Herpes zoster following BNT162b2 mRNA COVID-19 vaccination in patients with autoimmune inflammatory rheumatic diseases: a case series

evidence.text: All but one patient received antiviral treatment.
evidence.source: Herpes zoster following BNT162b2 mRNA COVID-19 vaccination in patients with autoimmune inflammatory rheumatic diseases: a case series

evidence.text: The study compared the incidence of SARS-CoV-2 infection in players and staff of the National Basketball Association (NBA) who did and did not receive a booster dose.
evidence.source: Association Between COVID-19 Booster Vaccination and Omicron Infection in a Highly Vaccinated Cohort of Players and Staff in the National Basketball Association

evidence.text: Five patients developed herpes zoster (HZ) within a short time after the first vaccine dose.
evidence.source: Herpes zoster following BNT162b2 mRNA COVID-19 vaccination in patients with autoimmune inflammatory rheumatic diseases: a case series

evidence.text: Six female patients with stable AIIRD developed herpes zoster (HZ) after the BNT162b2 mRNA vaccination.
evidence.source: Herpes zoster following BNT162b2 mRNA COVID-19 vaccination in patients with autoimmune inflammatory rheumatic diseases: a case series

evidence.text: Mager et al. demonstrated significant differences in the bacterial profiles of 40 oral cultivable species on soft and hard tissues in healthy subjects.
evidence.source: Defining the normal bacterial flora of the oral cavity

evidence.text: One patient developed herpes zoster (HZ) after the second vaccine dose.
evidence.source: Herpes zoster following BNT162b2 mRNA COVID-19 vaccination in patients with autoimmune inflammatory rheumatic diseases: a case series

evidence.text: Children with intellectual and developmental disabilities (IDDs) and children with medical complexity (CMC) have been disproportionally impacted by the coronavirus disease 2019 pandemic.
evidence.source: Supporting COVID-19 School Safety for Children With Disabilities and Medical Complexity

evidence.text: Five patients completed the second vaccine dose without other adverse effects.
evidence.source: Herpes zoster following BNT162b2 mRNA COVID-19 vaccination in patients with autoimmune inflammatory rheumatic diseases: a case series

evidence.text: Children with IDDs and CMC are at a higher risk of infectious complications and mortality.
evidence.source: Supporting COVID-19 School Safety for Children With Disabilities and Medical Complexity

evidence.text: An observational study monitored post-vaccination adverse effects in patients with autoimmune inflammatory rheumatic diseases (AIIRD) and controls.
evidence.source: Herpes zoster following BNT162b2 mRNA COVID-19 vaccination in patients with autoimmune inflammatory rheumatic diseases: a case series

evidence.text: At 16°C, energy metabolism was increased compared to 22°C.
evidence.source: Energy metabolism in humans at a lowered ambient temperature

evidence.text: The study was based on epidemiological, clinical, serological, and genomic analyses.
evidence.source: Coronavirus Disease 2019 (COVID-19) Re-infection by a Phylogenetically Distinct Severe Acute Respiratory Syndrome Coronavirus 2 Strain Confirmed by Whole Genome Sequencing

evidence.text: White-tailed deer (Odocoileus virginianus) are susceptible to SARS-CoV-2 infection.
evidence.source: Susceptibility of White-Tailed Deer (Odocoileus virginianus) to SARS-CoV-2

evidence.text: Manned space flight may lead to an increased risk of infection.
evidence.source: Impact of space flight on bacterial virulence and antibiotic susceptibility

evidence.text: Data from a massive online survey in the United States indicate an increased risk of COVID-19–related outcomes among respondents living with a child attending school in person.
evidence.source: Household COVID-19 risk and in-person schooling

evidence.text: The prevalence of herpes zoster (HZ) was 1.2% in patients with AIIRD.
evidence.source: Herpes zoster following BNT162b2 mRNA COVID-19 vaccination in patients with autoimmune inflammatory rheumatic diseases: a case series
